rulings.Case Title: COLLEEN A.R. YOU ET AL VS. JOHNSON & JOHNSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, January 21, 2025 in Department 304, Line 1. Defendant Longs Drug Stores California LLC's Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed. No Reply filed. Plaintiffs' complaint alleges that Ivan James You's ("Decedent") mesothelioma was a result of his family's use of Johnson's Baby Power ("Baby Powder") purchased at Defendant Longs Drug Stores California LLC's ("Defendant") stores from 1988 to 1992. Defendant moves for summary judgment, or in the alternative, summary adjudication. Defendant argues Plaintiffs have no evidence that Decedent obtained an asbestos-containing product from Defendant's stores or that any such alleged exposure was a substantial factor in causing his mesothelioma. Defendant also seeks summary adjudication as to the causes of action for: Negligence I-Survival, Products Liability-Survival, Negligence I-Wrongful Death, Products Liability-Wrongful Death, and Defendant's failure to inspect and test talcum power products. "[S]ummary judgment law in this state [no] longer require[s] a defendant moving for summary judgment to conclusively negate an element of the plaintiff's cause of action." (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal. 4th 826, 853.) "The defendant may also present evidence that the plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence." (Id. at 855.) A defendant may "base its summary judgment motion on plaintiffs' minimal discovery responses, contending both that its discovery, in the form of [a plaintiff's] deposition and special interrogatories, was comprehensive and that plaintiffs' factually devoid responses indicated that they could not prove causation." (Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal. App. 4th 96, 103.) In asbestos cases, a defendant's initial burden is met when "plaintiff's deposition testimony made clear that he had no knowledge himself that he was exposed to one of [defendant]'s asbestos-containing products" and interrogatory responses that "does not state specific facts showing that [plaintiff] was actually exposed to asbestos-containing material from [defendant]'s products." (Id. at 104.) Defendant argues Plaintiffs have no evidence that (1) Plaintiffs purchased the Baby Powder from Defendant's stores; (2) the Baby Powder used on Decedent or in Decedent's presence actually contained asbestos; and (3) the exposure from the Baby Powder obtained from Defendant was a substantial factor in causing Decedent's mesothelioma. (Motion at pp. 9-10.) However, Defendant's contention that Plaintiff's discovery responses are factually devoid (id. at 11-13) is demonstrably false. Defendant disregards, among other things, Plaintiff's extensive written interrogatory responses. (Defense Exhibit N.) Defendant also failed to depose Johnson & Johnson, the manufacturer of the Baby Powder, which Plaintiff identified as a key witness with knowledge of the asbestos content of the Baby Powder. (Exhibit N at 6.) Defendant therefore failed to shift the burden to Plaintiff, and its motion for summary judgment must be denied on that ground alone. (Tentative ruling continued, see next entry)

rulings.Case Title: COLLEEN A.R. YOU ET AL VS. JOHNSON & JOHNSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: LONGS DRUG STORES CALIFORNIA, LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR ISSUANCE OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (PART 2 OF 4) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry) First, Defendant contends that Plaintiffs have no evidence that Decedent was exposed to asbestos from the Baby Powder purchased from Defendant, citing Plaintiff Collen You's, the mother of Decedent, deposition testimony. The Court disagrees. As Plaintiffs noted in their Opposition, Ms. You testified the Baby Powder she used on and around Decedent was from Defendant stores. (Plaintiffs' Exhibit A at 83:8-22.) When asked if she purchased Baby Powder from any other locations, Ms. You testified, "basically, it was Longs Drugs then." (Id.) She even testified that she purchased Baby Powder from Longs from two possible store locations (1) El Camino Road in San Carlos, California; and (2) 42nd Street in San Mateo, California. (Id.) The fact that Ms. You did not recall specific instances when she purchased the Baby Powder from Defendant's stores or that she did not keep the containers of Baby Powder she used, does not create an inference of non-exposure or the inability to prove exposure through other sources of evidence than Plaintiff's testimony. To the contrary, even if the moving defendant carries its initial burden, "it is not uncommon for asbestos plaintiffs to rely on their own testimony or testimony by family members or coworkers, i.e., from individuals other than experts, or on business records detailing the purchase and placement of asbestos products at jobsites or in work environments. Even circumstantial evidence ... sufficient to support a reasonable inference of exposure may defeat summary judgment." (LAOSD Asbestos Cases (2020) 44 Cal.App.5th 475, 488-489 (cleaned up).) In any event, a plaintiff's ignorance is by itself insufficient to demonstrate that plaintiff lacks the ability to produce evidence supporting her claim and does not serve to shift defendant's initial burden. (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439 (emphasis added).) Second, Defendant argues Plaintiffs' discovery responses are factually devoid because they do not contain evidence that the Baby Powder Plaintiffs purchased from Defendant's stores contained asbestos. The Court disagrees. Ms. You's "ALL FACT" special interrogatory responses state, for example, that Ms. You bought Baby Powder from Defendant's store and used it on Decedent when he was a baby between 1988 and 1992. (Defense Exhibit N at 2:15-3:11.) That all Johnson & Johnson Baby Powder purchased from Defendant was manufactured with asbestos-containing talc and its inhalation was a substantial factor in causing Decedent's disease. (Id. at 2:15-4:9.) To support that claim, Plaintiffs identified exhibits that have been used in multiple trials against Johnson & Johnson to show its Baby Powder was manufactured with asbestos-containing talc, including advertisements, scientific studies, test results, pictures, samples, testimony, letters, mine studies, lab results, reports, and discovery responses. (Id. at 8:1-28:16.) The response then stated that Johnson & Johnson had knowledge of asbestos in its Baby Powder. (Id. at 38:17-45:28.) The discovery response also referred to Defendant's corporate representatives and Johnson & Johnson corporate representatives as persons with knowledge of the facts. (Id. at 6:25-7:13.) Plaintiffs identified numerous documents, including testimony, scientific studies, and reports showing that exposure to the asbestos-containing Baby Powder caused Decedent's disease. (Id. at 48:1-53:11.) Defendant failed to address the response and did not provide any contrary evidence. Defendant did not submit any expert testimony establishing that the Baby Powder was asbestos free or contained talc from asbestos-free mines. Nor did Defendant submit any other evidence establishing that Plaintiffs cannot obtain evidence of asbestos in the Baby Powder. (Tentative ruling continued, see next entry)

rulings.Case Title: COLLEEN A.R. YOU ET AL VS. JOHNSON & JOHNSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: LONGS DRUG STORES CALIFORNIA, LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR ISSUANCE OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (PART 4 OF 4) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry) The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 304 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on Friday, January 17, 2024, and state their intention to contest. If a hearing is requested, it will be on January 21, 2024, at 9:00 a.m. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029. Face coverings are optional. The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. = (304/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: COLLEEN A.R. YOU ET AL VS. JOHNSON & JOHNSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: LONGS DRUG STORES CALIFORNIA, LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR ISSUANCE OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (PART 3 OF 4) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry) Lastly, Defendant argues that Plaintiffs have no evidence that the exposure from the Baby Powder obtained from Defendant was a substantial factor in causing Decedent's mesothelioma. Even if Defendant had met its initial burden on this motion, which it has not, Plaintiffs' evidence establishes the existence of a disputed issue of fact. Plaintiffs filed an expert declaration from Dr. Richard Cohen stating that Ms. You's use of the asbestos-containing Baby Powder in Decedent's presence was a substantial factor in causing his mesothelioma and death. (Plaintiffs' Exhibit B at 79-80, 1-84.) Thus, Defendant fails to foreclose the possibility that Plaintiffs may yet prove asbestos exposure and causation attributed to Defendant. (See, e.g., Strobel v. Johnson & Johnson (2021) 70 Cal.App.5th 796, 802-803, 811-828 [reversing summary judgment where plaintiff presented conflicting expert declarations, including declaration of Dr. Cohen, which confirmed the presence of asbestos in the talcum ore Johnson & Johnson used to manufacture Johnson's Baby Powder]; Lyon v. Colgate-Palmolive Co. (2017) 16 Cal.App.5th 463, 471 [reversing summary judgment for defendant where plaintiff presented expert declaration confirming the presence of asbestos in mine sources and defendant's talcum powder product, which created triable issue].) Defendants Motion for Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication as to the First through Fourth Causes of Action is DENIED for the reasons discussed above. Defendant's motion for Summary Adjudication as to its alleged duty to test or inspect the products it sold is DENIED. The Court notes that Defendant identified the wrong party in its separate statement (i.e., Safeway). Regardless, the Court reviewed the arguments presented by both parties concerning Defendant's duty to inspect or test a product it sold. Defendant contends that its role does not include the duty to inspect or test products it sold, relying principally on Tourte v. Horton Manufacturing Company (1930) 108 Cal. App. 22. (Opening Brief, 22-23.) However, the continued viability of that dated authority is dubious. As Defendant concedes, Tourte was decided decades ago, long before the development of modern strict liability law. (See, e.g., Jiminez v. Superior Court (2002) 29 Cal.4th 473, 477 [noting that, a year after "California became the first state to allow recovery for strict products liability," Vandermark v. Ford Motor Co. (1964) 61 Cal.2d 256 "extended strict products liability to retailers" (cleaned up)].) "The theories of negligence and strict liability parallel and supplement each other, and the same policy considerations that militate for or against imposition of strict liability may apply with equal force in the context of negligence." (Bettencourt v. Hennessy Industries, Inc. (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 1103, 1118 (cleaned up).) While Defendant argues that the rule that a seller of products has no duty to inspect products manufactured by others for latent defects remains good law for purposes of negligence liability, that is insufficient to warrant summary adjudication. Even if that remains the law, Defendant presents no evidence that it did not know and had no reason to believe that the Baby Powder was defective. (Tentative ruling continued, see next entry)

rulings.Case Title: DOYLE BLANKENSHIP JR VS. GENERAL ELECTRIC COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Granting Preference In Setting Case For Trial, And Extending Discovery Cutoff
rulings.Rulings: (PART 2 OF 2) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry) 8. No later than February 4, 2025: a. Plaintiff must provide a statement to each Defendant stating the name and contact information of each lay person with knowledge of any facts supporting Plaintiff's claims as to each Defendant. b. Defendants must provide a statement stating the name and contact information of each lay person with knowledge of facts supporting Defendant's affirmative defenses. c. Any person not identified on the statements may be barred from testifying at trial or presenting a declaration on a summary judgment motion. 9. A further case management conference is set for March 25, 2025, at 4:00 p.m. a. Case management conference statements are not required. b. Parties may appear at the conference by zoom. c. If the parties agree that no CMC is necessary, they shall notify the Court at least two court days before the hearing. 10. Plaintiffs will send a settlement demand to Defendants no later than March 31, 2025. Response to the settlement demand is due April 14, 2025. The parties shall separately submit confidential settlement demands/offers. This confidential value must be separately submitted to Department503@sftc.org via electronic mail on April 21, 2025. 11. A mandatory settlement conference is set for Thursday, May 8, 2025, at 9:30 a.m. in Department 503. Personal appearance is required by all unless a party is excused for good cause. A request to excuse a personal appearance must be submitted to Department503@sftc.org two weeks before the mandatory settlement conference. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 304 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. = (304/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DOYLE BLANKENSHIP JR VS. GENERAL ELECTRIC COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Granting Preference In Setting Case For Trial, And Extending Discovery Cutoff
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, January 21, 2025 in Department 304, Line 2. Plaintiff's Motion for Order Granting Preference in Setting Case for Trial, and Extending Discovery Cutoff is GRANTED under C.C.P. 36(a). No opposition filed. 1. The trial date is May 12, 2025, at 11:15 a.m. in Department 206. a. Last day pursuant to C.C.P. section 36(f) is May 21, 2025. b. The parties shall follow the California Rules of Court, San Francisco Local Rules, and Local Rule 20. 2. The last day for hearing summary judgment/adjudication motions is May 6, 2025. a. Summary judgment/adjudication motions shall be brought on regular notice pursuant to the relevant provisions of the C.C.P., unless the parties stipulate otherwise. b. Before a party files and serves a summary judgment/adjudication motion, it must contact the clerk to make a reservation. c. The Court allows a maximum of four summary judgment/adjudication motions per day to be calendared, unless good cause is found to exceed this number. Contact the clerk to schedule a good cause hearing. 3. Time to respond to written discovery not yet served is shortened to 20 days. a. For written discovery that has already been served, responses are due within 20 days of this hearing or by the date determined by the C.C.P., whichever is earlier. b. Any issue/dispute that requires meet and confer, shall occur in person or via telephone, not by email or letter. 4. Electronic service is considered the equivalent of personal service. 5. The fact discovery cut-off date is April 25, 2025. 6. The expert discovery cut-off date is May 9, 2025. 7. All bankruptcy documents shall be turned over to defendants no later than February 20, 2025. a. If Plaintiff submits documents to bankruptcy trusts after this date, they must notify Defendants no later than five days after submission. b. The fact discovery cut-off does not apply to bankruptcy documents. (Tentative ruling continued, see next entry)
